title: Iterating Collections 🔁
description: Combine the power of Loops with Collections.

slides.title: The Perfect Match 🤝
slides.content:
# Loops + Collections

Remember the `for-in` loop? It was built specifically to work with Collections!

If you want to print every item in an Array, you just loop over it.

```swift
let cities = ["London", "Tokyo", "New York"]

for city in cities {
    print("I want to visit \(city)!")
}
```
Swift automatically runs the loop 3 times, pulling out each city one by one!

quizData.question: If an array has 50 items, modifying the 'for-in' loop to process all 50 items requires writing how much extra code compared to processing 3 items?

options.text: You must rewrite the loop entirely.

quizData.explanation: A for loop automatically adjusts to the size of the collection. It will comfortably run 3 times or 3 million times using the exact same code!

slides.title: Looping a Dictionary 📕
slides.content:
# Two Values at Once

Because a Dictionary has both a Key AND a Value, looping over it gives you a **Tuple** (a pair of values locked together in parentheses).

```swift
let grades = ["Alice": "A", "Bob": "C"]

for (student, grade) in grades {
    print("\(student) got a \(grade)")
}
```
We provide two variable names separated by a comma: `(student, grade)`.

quizData.question: When iterating a Dictionary returning `(name, age)`, which variable holds the Key?
quizData.explanation: A dictionary always returns exactly in the format `(Key, Value)`. Since 'name' came first, it represents the Key.

slides.title: See It in Action
slides.content: Look at how cleanly we can run math on every item in an Array!
